a: A vector is just a list of numbers that captures the meaning of something like a sentence, image, or product. A model reads your data and turns it into this list, so similar things end up with similar numbers. This number list is also called an embedding.
fs: A vector is a fixed-length list of floats produced by a model, where closeness in the numbers means closeness in meaning.
fj: A vector is not the raw text or a keyword tag; it is the numeric meaning the model assigns, so two different wordings can have nearly identical vectors.

a: A vector database is a store built to hold many vectors and find the ones most similar to a vector you give it. Instead of matching exact words, it matches by meaning. You put your data in as vectors, then ask it for the closest matches.
fs: A vector database stores embeddings and serves fast nearest-neighbor lookups by similarity rather than exact equality.
fj: It is not a replacement for a normal database; it specializes in similarity search, and many setups keep a regular database alongside it for the original text and fields.

a: Similarity is a single score that says how close two vectors are. Close vectors mean similar meaning; far apart vectors mean different meaning. A common measure is cosine similarity, which compares the direction the vectors point.
fs: Similarity is a math measure (like cosine or Euclidean distance) that turns two vectors into one closeness number.
fj: Higher is not always better: cosine similarity goes up for closer matches, but a distance score goes down for closer matches, so check which one your database returns.

a: Nearest neighbor search means: given one query vector, find the vectors closest to it. That is the core thing a vector database does. You usually ask for the top K closest matches, like the 5 most similar documents.
fs: Nearest neighbor search returns the top-K most similar vectors to a query by ranking on a distance or similarity score.
fj: Real systems rarely check every vector exactly; they use approximate (ANN) search that is far faster and may miss a match now and then in exchange for speed.

q: What is an index in a vector database?
a: An index is the extra structure the database builds so it can find close vectors fast. Without it, every search would compare your query against all vectors one by one. The index pre-organizes the vectors so most can be skipped during a search.
fs: An index (like HNSW or IVF) organizes vectors ahead of time to make nearest-neighbor search fast instead of scanning everything.
fj: An index is not the data itself; it is a helper built from your vectors, so adding new vectors can mean the index must update before they show up in results.
